result.question: How can a $55 SO-101 arm (LeRobot, ACT, 2x 640x480 cams, no force sensing) pick and sort recyclable water bottles and caps by plastic type (PET vs HDPE vs PP etc.) for easier downstream processing in Vietnam/HCMC context? Cover: (a) plastic-type identification techniques feasible on cheap hardware — computer vision (RGB, resin codes, color/shape priors), NIR spectroscopy limits for dark/clear plastics, cheap sensor add-ons (load cells, capacitive, float/sink), hybrid CV+heuristics; (b) what commercial MRF/robotic sorters (AMP Robotics, ZenRobotics, TOMRA) do and what transfers to a hobby arm; (c) training approaches — ACT imitation learning vs decoupled perception (classifier picks bin, policy does grasp), datasets, YOLO/segmentation fine-tuning on custom bottle/cap data; (d) grasp physics for bottles (crushed/intact, labels on/off) and caps within 0.2-0.3kg payload and 35-40cm reach; (e) Vietnam-specific: ve chai economics, which plastic separations actually add value (cap HDPE/PP at 20-35k VND/kg vs PET 4-7k), realistic scoping per the existing docs/vietnam_applications.md skeptic findings. Output: an actionable, adversarially-verified build plan for the task.
result.summary: Synthesis step was skipped or failed — returning 10 verified claims unmerged.

confirmed.claim: Even with the best of ten ML methods (PCA-CNN), the cheap 18-channel sensor only achieves 72.50% mean classification accuracy across the six plastic types — far below commercial NIR sorter performance, so it cannot be relied on as a sole discriminator.
confirmed.quote: Convolutional Neural Networks and Multi-Layer Perceptron can recognise plastics with a mean accuracy of 72.50% and 70.25%, respectively

confirmed.claim: PET is the worst-recognized plastic (66% accuracy) with this low-cost sensor, and the largest confusions are exactly the pairs a bottle/cap sorter cares about: PET vs PVC and HDPE vs PP — meaning cheap NIR does not cleanly separate cap plastics (HDPE/PP) from each other.
confirmed.quote: the smallest accuracy of 66% for PET plastic ... the largest errors are presented for PET being confused with PVC, HDPE with PP

confirmed.claim: The review identifies limited real-world datasets, scalability issues, and environmental variability as the key challenges for AI-based plastic classification — directly relevant to deploying a hobby-scale sorter on messy Vietnamese ve chai streams rather than lab benches.
confirmed.quote: The review highlights the accuracy of identification and classification methods for plastic waste and identifies key challenges, including limited real-world datasets, scalability issues, and environmental variability.

confirmed.claim: Per the article's own caveat (surfaced in search-result text from the ScienceDirect page; not present in the Semantic Scholar abstract copy), reported high classification accuracies come from controlled conditions and restricted datasets, so published accuracy figures should be discounted when scoping a real-world build.
confirmed.quote: Although high classification accuracies are reported, they are typically achieved under controlled conditions with restricted datasets that may not capture real-world complexities

confirmed.claim: The WaDaBa dataset used comprises 4,000 images of household plastic waste across 5 resin categories, collected over four months, with severe class imbalance (PET: 2,200 images vs Other: 40) — indicating a hobby-scale custom dataset (hundreds to low thousands of images) is in the right regime for this task.
confirmed.quote: Objects...acquired from households over a period of four months.

confirmed.claim: The 99%+ accuracies are likely optimistic for real deployment: the 80:10:10 split was done per-class on images, with no stated safeguard against the same physical object (photographed at multiple angles/lighting) appearing in both train and test sets, i.e., probable object-level data leakage.
confirmed.quote: data is split in an 80:10:10 ratio for the training, validation, and test set respectively

confirmed.claim: The study's resin classes are ABS, HDPE, PS, and PVC from construction/demolition waste — it does NOT include PET or PP and does not test beverage bottles, so its results transfer only partially to the bottle/cap sorting task (HDPE is covered; the key PET-vs-PP-vs-HDPE cap distinction is not directly validated).
confirmed.quote: The dataset comprises four commonly used plastic types in construction projects—ABS, HDPE, PS, and PVC.

confirmed.claim: Black polymers are traditionally undetectable by standard optical sorting sensors (NIR-based), and TOMRA requires optional add-on technologies beyond its base AUTOSORT sensor configuration to identify them — confirming that dark/black plastics defeat conventional NIR sorting.
confirmed.quote: Optional add-on technologies expand material identification capabilities for traditionally undetectable fractions like black polymers

confirmed.claim: AMP Robotics' commercial sorting AI (Neuron platform) identifies recyclables using visual features from cameras — colors, textures, shapes, sizes, patterns, and brand labels — rather than NIR spectroscopy, meaning RGB-camera-based classification is the core sensing modality used by a leading commercial robotic sorter and is in principle the same modality available on a cheap 640x480-camera hobby arm.
confirmed.quote: The AMP Neuron™ AI platform continuously trains itself by recognizing different colors, textures, shapes, sizes, patterns, and even brand labels.

confirmed.claim: ACT trained via imitation learning (100 teleoperated demos per task) on the SO-101 achieved 75% success on simple pick-and-place (Pen Transfer) but 0% success on the selective sorting task requiring object identification among distractors (Selective Color Sorting), directly showing that end-to-end ACT fails at the identify-then-sort structure the bottle/cap task requires.
confirmed.quote: ACT exhibits extremely high rates of grasp instability, repetition loops, and state mismatch, indicating failures across both perception and action consistency.

refuted.claim: A ~GBP 56 (~USD 70) SparkFun Triad Spectroscopy Sensor (AS7265x, 18 channels spanning 410-940 nm visible-to-NIR) can classify six household plastic types (PET, HDPE, PVC, LDPE, PP, PS), making spectroscopy-based plastic ID a plausible cheap sensor add-on for a hobby-budget sorter.

The current state of the art in plastic waste classification pairs deep-learning vision models (CNNs, YOLO architectures, Transformers) with spectroscopic sensing (NIR, FTIR, Raman) — i.e., pure RGB vision is routinely augmented by spectroscopy in the literature, not used alone.

The review's recommended future directions are lightweight models, multi-sensor fusion, and edge-AI deployment — supporting the feasibility premise that low-cost edge hardware (like an SO-101 with cheap cams plus auxiliary sensors) is where the field is heading.

A Siamese network with only 4 convolutional layers, trained on RGB images resized to 105x105, achieved ~99.7% accuracy classifying household plastic waste into 5 resin-code categories (PET, PE-HD, PP, PS, Other) on the WaDaBa dataset — suggesting resin-code-level classification from plain RGB is achievable with very lightweight models suitable for cheap hardware.

Peer-reviewed research demonstrates that deep learning on plain RGB images (no NIR/hyperspectral hardware) can classify rigid plastic waste by resin type, and the authors conclude RGB is a practical low-cost alternative to costly spectroscopic identification systems — directly supporting the feasibility of camera-only plastic-type ID on a $55 arm's 640x480 webcams.

unverified.claim: Selective sorting (pick the correct object among distractors) was the hardest benchmark task on SO-101 for every evaluated policy, with all methods — ACT, SmolVLA, Wall-X, and pi-0.5 — scoring only 0-10% success, versus 70-95% for plain pick-and-place transfer.

On the low-cost SO-101, execution instability (grasp instability and repetition loops) — not semantic misunderstanding — is the dominant failure source across all policies, driven by hardware limits such as actuator noise, joint backlash, and imperfect camera-to-action calibration.

A robotic waste sorter can sort recyclables using decoupled perception (an SSD object detector trained on autonomously collected images) plus scripted manipulation primitives, rather than end-to-end imitation learning — supporting the 'classifier picks bin, policy/primitive does grasp' architecture for the SO-101 project.

The system classifies waste only at the material-category level (aluminum can, glass bottle, plastic bottle) and does NOT distinguish plastic resin types (PET vs HDPE vs PP) from RGB vision — evidence that even academic RGB-based sorters treat 'plastic bottle' as a single class, and resin-level sorting needs priors or other sensing.

Graspless 'push-and-drop' manipulation (pushing the item off the surface into a bin) achieves a faster cycle time (avg 3.3 s) than pick-and-release with a soft five-finger gripper (avg 5.2 s) — directly relevant to a low-payload SO-101 handling crushed/intact bottles, since pushing avoids grasp-force and payload limits entirely.

A 135° V-shaped fingertip profile on a 3D-printed two-finger gripper achieved a 100% grasp success rate on rigid 66 mm PET bottles (24–71 mm diameter range), whereas a conventional 45° V-profile produced only unstable flat-surface contact and failed to securely grasp bottles of that size.

A single hobby-grade servomotor (Hitec HS-945MG, 1.079 N·m at 6.0 V) driving a four-bar parallel linkage in PLA is sufficient to grasp and retain waste objects up to 0.6 kg with an FEA-validated safety factor of 3.5, at an in-house material cost of USD 20–40 and about 10 hours of desktop 3D-printer time.

Deformable waste items (plastic bags, crushed/soft objects) are substantially harder to grasp than rigid ones with a rigid parallel-jaw gripper: success dropped from 100% on rigid objects to 40–60% on deformables, implying crushed bottles will be a distinct failure mode versus intact ones.

PET beverage bottles in Vietnam already have a relatively high collection-for-recycling rate (45-65%; PET packaging overall ~50%, the highest of all resins), meaning intact PET bottles are already efficiently captured by existing collection — a hobby sorter adds little value on the bottle-identification step itself.

Separating caps and sleeves (made of HDPE and PP) from PET bottles is an established, necessary step in Vietnam's PET recycling chain — the study's PET recycling figures are computed only after these other-resin contaminants are removed, confirming cap/bottle separation is a real value-adding operation.
